entries.title: Senna (Cassia angustifolia)
entries.summary: Botanical monograph for Senna (Cassia angustifolia), an Indian shrub of the Caesalpinaceae family whose sennoside-rich leaves, pods and flowers provide a proven laxative action by stimulating colonic peristalsis; offered by GreenSpace Herbs as a standardized botanical extract for B2B supply.
facts: Cassia angustifolia (Senna) is a much-branching erect shrub of the family Caesalpinaceae.
It grows both wild and under cultivation across many parts of India, a principal source of the leaf and pod material.
The active compounds responsible for its laxative properties are the sennosides, found in the leaves, pods and flowers.
The extract is standardized to sennosides, the marker actives.
Senna increases the peristaltic movements of the colon by a local action directly upon the intestinal wall.
Parts used are the leaves, pods and flowers.
Beyond its principal role as a laxative, Senna has traditionally been used as an expectorant, wound dresser, antidysenteric and carminative.
Available from GreenSpace Herbs as a standardized botanical extract for B2B supply.

qa.q: What is Senna (Cassia angustifolia) used for?
qa.a: It is best known as a natural laxative that stimulates colonic peristalsis to relieve constipation, and has also traditionally been used as an expectorant, wound dresser, antidysenteric and carminative.

qa.q: What are the active compounds in Senna?
qa.a: The sennosides, found in the leaves, pods and flowers, are the marker actives responsible for Senna's laxative properties, and the extract is standardized to them.

qa.q: How does Senna work as a laxative?
qa.a: It increases the peristaltic movements of the colon by a local action directly upon the intestinal wall, promoting bowel movement and relieving constipation.

entries.title: Cassia angustifolia (Senna): The Science Behind Sennosides and Its Laxative Power
entries.summary: Science article explaining how Cassia angustifolia (Senna) sennosides A-D act as a prodrug converted by colonic bacteria into rhein anthrone, driving a dual mechanism of increased motility and enhanced water secretion to relieve constipation.
facts: Cassia angustifolia is also known as Indian Senna, Tinnevelly Senna or Senna Maki, a shrub of the family Fabaceae.
Its laxative effect is driven by glycosides Sennosides A, B, C and D, which are dianthrone glycosides of linked anthraquinone units attached to sugar moieties.
Sennoside A & B are the principal agents responsible for cathartic activity; Sennoside C & D are present in smaller amounts.
Less than 10% of sennosides are absorbed from the gut; they act as a prodrug.
Intestinal bacteria in the colon convert sennosides into the active metabolite rhein anthrone, which enhances peristaltic movement and accelerates intestinal transit.

Rhein anthrone stimulates colonic macrophages to release prostaglandin E2 (PGE2), which downregulates aquaporin AQP3 (reducing water reabsorption) and promotes electrolyte secretion, drawing water into the bowel.
Senna's effectiveness comes from a dual mechanism: increased motility combined with enhanced water secretion, producing softer, bulkier stool.
Sennoside concentration varies with plant maturity, environment and processing; younger leaves and pods have higher content and proper drying is essential (Franz, 1993).
Rare severe reactions such as anaphylaxis have been reported with Cassia-containing herbal products, and drug-herb interactions should be considered.

qa.q: How do sennosides in Senna actually work?
qa.a: Sennosides act as a prodrug: less than 10% are absorbed, and colonic bacteria convert them into rhein anthrone, which increases colonic motility and, via PGE2 release, reduces water reabsorption (downregulating aquaporin AQP3) and promotes electrolyte secretion, drawing water into the bowel for softer stool.

qa.q: Which sennosides are most important for the laxative effect?
qa.a: Sennosides A and B are the principal agents responsible for cathartic activity, while Sennosides C and D are present in smaller amounts and contribute to the overall effect.

qa.q: Is Senna safe?
qa.a: Its prodrug design limits action to the large intestine and minimizes systemic exposure, but rare severe reactions such as anaphylaxis have been reported with Cassia-containing products, so clinicians and formulators should account for potential drug-herb interactions in sensitive populations.

entries.title: Senna (Cassia angustifolia)
entries.summary: Botanical monograph for Senna (Cassia angustifolia Vahl.), a sennoside-rich herbal laxative available from GreenSpace Herbs as a standardized botanical extract for B2B supply, covering its phytochemistry, digestive benefits, safe use and clinical evidence.
facts: Latin name Cassia angustifolia Vahl., family Caesalpinaceae; in Ayurveda called Swarnapatri or Sona Mukhi and classified under Virechana Dravyas (herbs for therapeutic purgation).
The dried leaflets and seed pods are the main parts used medicinally and contain anthraquinone glycosides that stimulate bowel movement.
Key active compounds are Sennosides A and B (the main laxative agents), plus rhein, aloe-emodin and chrysophanol, along with flavonoids and tannins.
Stimulates bowel contraction within 6 to 12 hours; often taken at bedtime to induce morning relief.
Sennosides act by stimulating enteric nerves and reducing water reabsorption in the colon, leading to bowel evacuation.
Senna is one of the few herbal laxatives with FDA approval and widespread clinical validation for short-term use.
Used before colonoscopies and abdominal surgeries to clear the bowel, often combined with osmotic laxatives like polyethylene glycol.
Grows in arid and semi-arid zones including southern India (Tamil Nadu, Rajasthan), northeast Africa (Sudan, Egypt) and the Arabian Peninsula.
Offered as a standardized botanical extract (standardized for sennoside content) for B2B supply; enquiries via /contact-us/.

Precautions: avoid in pregnancy/breastfeeding unless prescribed, in children under 12 except pediatric forms, and in IBD, Crohn's, ulcerative colitis, or kidney/heart conditions; long-term use can cause dependency and electrolyte imbalance.

qa.q: What is Senna used for?
qa.a: Senna (Cassia angustifolia) is a herbal stimulant laxative used for short-term relief of occasional constipation and digestive cleansing. Its sennosides stimulate bowel contraction within 6 to 12 hours, and it is also used for bowel preparation before colonoscopies and surgery.

qa.q: What are the active compounds in Senna?
qa.a: The main laxative agents are Sennosides A and B, supported by other anthraquinones (rhein, aloe-emodin, chrysophanol) plus flavonoids and tannins. GreenSpace Herbs supplies it as an extract standardized for sennoside content.

qa.q: Is Senna safe to use long term?
qa.a: Senna is best used short-term at recommended doses. Long-term or chronic use can lead to dependency and electrolyte imbalances. It should be avoided or used only under guidance in pregnancy, breastfeeding, children under 12, inflammatory bowel disease, and kidney or heart conditions.

qa.q: Can I buy Senna extract from GreenSpace Herbs?
qa.a: Yes. Senna is available as a standardized botanical extract for B2B supply. Enquiries can be made through the contact page at /contact-us/.

entries.title: Coleus (Coleus forskohlii (Willd.) Briq.)
entries.summary: Monograph for Coleus forskohlii (Gandira), a standardized botanical extract rich in the diterpenoid forskolin, clinically studied for weight management, cardiac function, glaucoma and metabolic health. Offered by GreenSpace Herbs as a B2B standardized extract.
facts: Coleus forskohlii (Willd.) Briq. is of the Lamiaceae family; the part used is the roots (tubers).
Common names include Gandira (Sanskrit), Patharchur (Hindi), Manganiperu (Kannada) and Marunthu koorkan (Tamil).
The principal bioactive is forskolin, a diterpenoid that activates cellular cyclic AMP (cAMP) pathways; extracts are standardized to a defined forskolin content.
Supporting diterpenoids include barbatusin and coleon O, plus ferruginol, sugiol and manoyl oxide.
For weight management, forskolin stimulates lipolysis by activating adenylate cyclase and raising intracellular cAMP, promoting fat metabolism.

It shows anti-glaucoma activity by decreasing intraocular pressure through enhanced aqueous-humor outflow, cardioprotective effects improving myocardial contractility and cardiac output, and anti-vitiligo activity stimulating melanocyte proliferation.
Native to India, it thrives in the lower Himalayan belt and southern states such as Karnataka and Tamil Nadu.
In Ayurveda it targets Kapha imbalance and metabolic stagnation, and is blended synergistically with Salacia reticulata and Sesamum indicum for metabolic regulation.
Cited research includes Bhowal & Mehta 2017, Loftus et al. 2015, Badmaev et al. 2015, Mutolo et al. 2016, Majeed et al. 2015, Ahsan et al. 2014 and Diehl et al. 2021.
Responsibly cultivated via contract farming in Southern India using pesticide-free, rainfed environments; third-party lab tested for purity, identity and contaminant screening.

qa.q: What is Coleus forskohlii used for?
qa.a: Coleus forskohlii (Gandira) is a standardized botanical extract rich in forskolin, used for weight management and fat metabolism, cardiovascular support, metabolic health, glaucoma (lowering intraocular pressure), and vitiligo repigmentation. Forskolin works by activating cellular cyclic AMP (cAMP) pathways.

qa.q: What is forskolin and how does it work?
qa.a: Forskolin is the principal diterpenoid in Coleus forskohlii roots. It activates adenylate cyclase and raises intracellular cyclic AMP (cAMP), which stimulates lipolysis (fat metabolism), improves myocardial contractility, and enhances aqueous-humor outflow in the eye. GreenSpace's extract is standardized to a defined forskolin content.

qa.q: Can I source Coleus forskohlii extract from GreenSpace Herbs?
qa.a: Yes. GreenSpace Herbs offers Coleus forskohlii as a standardized botanical extract for B2B supply. It is third-party lab tested for purity, identity and contaminants; enquire via the Contact Us page.

entries.title: Herbal Extracts (For Consumers)
entries.summary: Consumer-facing overview of GreenSpace Herbs' standardized, energized botanical extracts — explaining why standardized extracts beat whole herbs, how EASI energized actives improve bioavailability, and showcasing featured Ayurvedic botanicals.
facts: GreenSpace argues a standardized extract equivalent in ingredient composition ratios to the whole herb is enriched with phytochemical constituents, giving superior active principles and flexible dosing.
Bio-assays are described as the ultimate tool to establish therapeutic efficacy; every GreenSpace extract is profiled and quality-assured before formulation.
GreenSpace delivers clinically validated Energized Active Supplement Ingredients (EASI) by leveraging AI, Quantum Chemistry and Ayurveda.

Claimed consumer benefits of energized actives: higher bioavailability, sustained plasma presence for longer-lasting support, preserved whole-herb integrity, and clinical validation via human pharmacokinetic studies.

Featured botanicals include Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera, adaptogen), Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri, nootropic), Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia, immunity), Haritaki (Terminalia chebula, antioxidant), Amla (Emblica officinalis, vitamin C source) and Tulsi (Ocimum sanctum, wellness herb).

entries.title: Crystal Frequencies: Supplements That Literally Vibe With You (Blog)
entries.summary: A playful science blog post explaining GreenSpace's 'Crystal Frequencies' concept — sound-tuning plant extracts into micro crystals that store a vibration so the body recognizes and uses them more efficiently, plus the physics, Ayurvedic Prabhava link, benefits and caveats.
facts: Crystal Frequencies turns plant extracts into micro crystals that carry a tuned vibration, so the body recognizes the supplement and uses it more efficiently; the project was initially misnamed 'Frozen Frequencies'.

The process exposes liquid extract to organ-specific frequencies in an echo-free chamber (bass for digestion, mids for the vagus nerve, highs for the cortex), then grows ~10-micron crystals via controlled nucleation, low-temperature vacuum drying, and nitrogen-filled blister packs.
The stated takeaway is that a crystal-tuned capsule can hit goals with about 25% less material, benefiting cost and sustainability.
Studies to date were small (roughly senior-project sized); larger double-blind trials with sham-tuned controls are described as in the works.
It links the tech to the Ayurvedic concept of Prabhava — an herb's special potency beyond flavor or chemistry — suggesting crystallization gives that idea physical scaffolding.
Lattice integrity is verified with Raman spectroscopy and photoluminescence; the piece notes the FDA still needs convincing and labels must stay honest.
The article is authored/quoted by 'Shafi, The Quantum Ayurveda Man' and tagged as Science, 2026.

qa.q: What is Crystal Frequencies?
qa.a: Crystal Frequencies is a GreenSpace concept that sound-tunes herbal extracts (like fenugreek, Ashwagandha and turmeric) and crystallizes them into ~10-micron micro crystals carrying a tuned vibration, so the body 'recognizes' the supplement and uses it more efficiently — claimed to hit goals with roughly 25% less material.

qa.q: Is Crystal Frequencies scientifically proven?
qa.a: Not yet fully. The article says the concept stands on physics like wave-particle duality and resonance, but existing studies were small and larger double-blind trials with sham-tuned controls are still needed; regulators like the FDA have no standard test for 'frequency' and medical claims require peer-reviewed proof.

qa.q: Why crystals instead of freezing?
qa.a: The post clarifies the effect comes from crystallization, not freezing. Ice is only a temporary grid of water molecules, whereas crystals lock atoms into a repeating 3D lattice that acts as 'nature's flash drive' to store the tuned vibration, and no cryogenic deep freeze is required.

entries.title: Gymnema sylvestre (Madhunashini / Gurmar)
entries.summary: Botanical extract monograph for Gymnema sylvestre (Madhunashini), the Ayurvedic 'sugar destroyer' — a standardized gymnemic-acid leaf extract for blood-sugar regulation, insulin and pancreatic support, and metabolic health.
facts: Gymnema sylvestre is known in Ayurveda as Madhunashini ('destroyer of sugar') and commonly as Gurmar.
Family: Asclepiadaceae (now reclassified under Apocynaceae); the leaves are the primary medicinal part.
It is classified in Ayurveda as Medohara (fat-reducing) and Pramehahara (anti-diabetic), with a Tikta (bitter) taste and Ushna Virya (heating energy), balancing Kapha and Vata doshas.

Key active compounds are gymnemic acids (triterpenoid saponins that suppress sweet taste and delay glucose absorption) and gurmarin (a polypeptide that blocks sugar receptors on the tongue), plus flavonoids, alkaloids, betaine, stigmasterol, tannins and phytosterols.
Standardized extracts typically contain 25–75% gymnemic acids.

Gymnemic acids inhibit intestinal glucose absorption and reduce blood glucose in both Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes; the herb stimulates regeneration of pancreatic β-cells and increases insulin sensitivity.
It decreases serum triglycerides, LDL and total cholesterol, supporting weight and lipid management.
Studies confirm gymnemic acids suppress sweet-taste perception for up to 90 minutes.
Precautions: hypoglycemia risk when combined with antidiabetic medications; avoid in pregnancy/lactation without prescription; discontinue two weeks before surgery.
Native range spans central and southern India, Sri Lanka, Southeast Asia, and parts of tropical Africa and Australia.

qa.q: What does Gymnema sylvestre (Madhunashini) do?
qa.a: It regulates blood sugar by inhibiting intestinal glucose absorption, stimulating pancreatic β-cell regeneration, increasing insulin sensitivity, and reducing sugar cravings by temporarily suppressing sweet taste — supporting diabetes care, weight and lipid management.

qa.q: What is Gymnema standardized to?
qa.a: GreenSpace supplies it as a standardized leaf extract containing 25–75% gymnemic acids, its signature triterpenoid saponins.

qa.q: Is Gymnema safe with diabetes medication?
qa.a: It can enhance the effects of antidiabetic medications, so blood sugar should be monitored closely (especially with insulin or oral antidiabetics), and it should be discontinued about two weeks before surgery.

facts: Gymnema sylvestre R.Br. (Madhunashini) is a slender perennial woody climber of the family Asclepiadaceae (now reclassified under Apocynaceae).

It grows through the Deccan Peninsula and Western Ghats, extending into northern and western India, Sri Lanka, Southeast Asia, and parts of tropical Africa and Australia; it grows from sea level to 800 metres with 800–1500 mm annual rainfall.
The leaves are the primary medicinal part; an aqueous leaf extract contains gymnemic acid III, IV, V, VIII and IX.
Key active compounds: gymnemic acids (triterpenoid saponins), gurmarin (polypeptide blocking sweet-taste receptors), plus flavonoids, alkaloids, betaine, stigmasterol, tannins and phytosterols.
Gymnemic acid exerts anti-diabetic action by lowering plasma glucose and serum insulin through regeneration of insulin-producing beta cells, a mechanism relevant even in Type 1 diabetes.
Standardized extracts typically contain 25–75% gymnemic acids.
Studies show it reduces fasting and postprandial glucose, enhances insulin secretion, lowers serum triglycerides/LDL/total cholesterol, and suppresses sweet-taste perception for up to 90 minutes.
Precautions include hypoglycemia risk with antidiabetic drugs, limited pregnancy/lactation safety data, and discontinuing two weeks before surgery.

qa.q: What family and botanical authority does Madhunashini belong to?
qa.a: It is Gymnema sylvestre R.Br., of the family Asclepiadaceae (now reclassified under Apocynaceae), an Ayurvedic woody climber known as the 'sugar destroyer.'

qa.q: Which gymnemic acids are in the leaf extract?
qa.a: An aqueous extract of the leaves contains gymnemic acid III, IV, V, VIII and IX, standardized commercially to 25–75% total gymnemic acids.

qa.q: How does Madhunashini help with blood sugar?
qa.a: Its gymnemic acids inhibit intestinal glucose absorption and delay carbohydrate digestion, while lowering plasma glucose and serum insulin through regeneration of insulin-producing β-cells, supporting both Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes.

facts: Gymnema sylvestre (Gurmar / Madhunashini) is a climbing herb native to the tropical forests of India and Africa, and one of the most researched botanicals in metabolic health science.
Its active compounds are triterpenoid saponins called gymnemic acids, structurally similar to glucose.
Three primary mechanisms: suppressing sweetness by binding sweet taste receptors, blocking glucose transporters in the small intestine, and moderating the post-meal glucose load.

Human clinical trials, including landmark studies by Shanmugasundaram et al. (1990), showed meaningful reductions in fasting blood glucose and HbA1c in Type 2 diabetes subjects over 18–24 months of standardized Gymnema extract supplementation.
Gymnemic acid content is typically standardized to 25% or 75%, determining extract potency and consistency.
GreenSpace Herbs supplies HPLC-standardized Gymnema extract across the 25%–75% gymnemic acid range for reliable batch-to-batch formulation.

qa.q: How does Gymnema sylvestre work?
qa.a: Its gymnemic acids (triterpenoid saponins resembling glucose) suppress sweet taste on the tongue, block glucose transporters in the small intestine to reduce sugar absorption, and thereby moderate the post-meal glucose surge.

qa.q: Is Gymnema clinically supported for blood sugar?
qa.a: Yes — human trials including landmark work by Shanmugasundaram et al. (1990) showed meaningful reductions in fasting blood glucose and HbA1c in Type 2 diabetes subjects over 18–24 months of standardized Gymnema supplementation.

facts: Amla (Emblica officinalis Gaertn., syn. Phyllanthus emblica) is a Euphorbiaceae-family fruit and one of the richest natural sources of vitamin C.
In Ayurveda Amla is classified as Tridosha hara — balancing Vata, Pitta and Kapha — and is the primary ingredient in classical formulations Chyawanprash and Triphala.

Key bioactive constituents include vitamin C (ascorbic acid), tannins (Emblicanin A & B, punigluconin, pedunculagin), flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol), and phenolic acids (gallic, ellagic, phyllembelic acid).
The fruit is the primary part used medicinally; seeds, leaves and bark also have therapeutic value.
Emblicanin A and B have been shown to enhance superoxide dismutase (SOD) and glutathione levels.
Randomized controlled trials found Amla supplementation significantly reduced total cholesterol and LDL levels, and Amla extract improved fasting and post-meal glucose in Type 2 diabetics.
It is cultivated widely in India (Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, Tamil Nadu) and across Nepal, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh and parts of China and Southeast Asia.
Precautions: high acidity may affect sensitive teeth, high tannins can interfere with iron absorption, and diabetics on medication should monitor blood sugar to avoid hypoglycemia.
GreenSpace Herbs offers Amla as a standardized botanical extract (standardized to tannins / vitamin C) for B2B supply.

qa.q: What does Amla do?
qa.a: Amla (Emblica officinalis) is an Ayurvedic Rasayana rejuvenative rich in vitamin C and antioxidants. It supports immunity, promotes collagen synthesis for skin, strengthens hair, aids digestion and liver/metabolic health, and has been shown in trials to lower total cholesterol and LDL and improve glycemic control in Type 2 diabetics.

qa.q: Is Amla the same as Indian Gooseberry and Amalaki?
qa.a: Yes. Amla, Indian Gooseberry, and the Sanskrit name Amalaki all refer to Emblica officinalis Gaertn. (synonym Phyllanthus emblica), a fruit in the Euphorbiaceae family.

qa.q: How is GreenSpace Herbs' Amla extract standardized?
qa.a: It is offered as a standardized botanical extract calibrated to its tannin and vitamin C content, available for B2B supply — enquiries via the contact page.

qa.q: Are there any precautions with Amla?
qa.a: Amla is very well tolerated, but its high acidity may bother sensitive teeth, its tannins can reduce iron absorption (avoid taking with iron supplements), and diabetics on medication should monitor blood sugar to prevent hypoglycemia.

facts: Garcinia cambogia (Garcinia gummi-gutta), Clusiaceae family, is a small tropical fruit whose pumpkin-shaped rind concentrates hydroxycitric acid (HCA).
HCA is believed to inhibit ATP citrate lyase, a key enzyme in fatty-acid synthesis, and may regulate appetite by modulating serotonin levels.
Extracts are standardized to 50–60% HCA.

A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial (Journal of Obesity, 2011) showed statistically significant reductions in body weight and BMI in the group receiving 1500 mg/day of HCA for 12 weeks.
Recommended dosage is 500–1500 mg/day in divided doses taken before meals, in capsules, tablets, powders and functional beverages.

Regulatory status: permitted as a dietary ingredient under DSHEA in the USA; usable in EU food supplements though weight-loss claims require EFSA substantiation; approved under FSSAI as a nutraceutical ingredient in India.
Sourced primarily from India, Indonesia and Sri Lanka.
Generally recognized as safe within recommended dosages; high doses may interact with serotonin-affecting medications.

qa.q: What is Garcinia cambogia used for?
qa.a: It is best known as the botanical source of hydroxycitric acid (HCA), used in weight-loss and obesity-management formulations and metabolic-health products targeting lipid profiles and insulin sensitivity. HCA inhibits ATP citrate lyase to limit fat synthesis and enhances satiety via serotonin.

qa.q: Is Garcinia cambogia clinically tested?
qa.a: Yes. A 2011 randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in the Journal of Obesity showed statistically significant reductions in body weight and BMI at 1500 mg/day of HCA over 12 weeks, and other trials indicated appetite-suppressing and lipid-profile benefits, though results vary with standardization and study design.

qa.q: What HCA standardization does GreenSpace supply?
qa.a: GreenSpace Herbs supplies Garcinia cambogia extract standardized to 50–60% HCA for B2B formulation use.

qa.q: Is Garcinia cambogia safe?
qa.a: It is generally recognized as safe within recommended dosages (500–1500 mg/day before meals). Mild digestive discomfort may occur in sensitive individuals, and high doses may interact with medications affecting serotonin levels.

facts: Garcinia cambogia Desr., known in Ayurveda as Vrikshamla or Malabar Tamarind, is a medium-sized evergreen tree of the Guttiferae (Clusiaceae) family.
The fruit rind (pericarp) is the primary part used; in Ayurveda it is classed as Amla (sour) and Laghu (light), used as a digestive, carminative and fat-reducing botanical.
Hydroxycitric acid (HCA) is the primary active compound, present at up to 30% in the dried rind; other constituents include citric acid, polyphenols, flavonoids and garcinol.
HCA works by inhibiting ATP citrate lyase, reducing conversion of excess carbohydrates into fat (lipogenesis) and increasing satiety, and raises serotonin to curb appetite.
Garcinol is an anti-inflammatory and antioxidant constituent shown to inhibit COX-2 in laboratory studies.
Randomized controlled trials demonstrated modest weight loss and reduced BMI with Garcinia extract at HCA 1500–2800 mg/day.
Standardized extracts are calibrated to 50–60% HCA; forms include dried rind, capsules, tablets, teas and syrups, often combined with Triphala or Guggulu.
Grows across the Western Ghats of India (Kerala, Karnataka, Maharashtra), Indonesia, Thailand, Myanmar, Sri Lanka and parts of Central and West Africa.
Precautions: avoid in pregnancy/lactation, monitor blood sugar in diabetics, may interact with SSRIs/mood stabilizers via serotonin, and use with caution in liver disorders.

qa.q: What is Vrikshamla?
qa.a: Vrikshamla (also called Malabar Tamarind) is the Ayurvedic name for Garcinia cambogia Desr., an evergreen tree of the Guttiferae/Clusiaceae family. Its fruit rind is used traditionally as a digestive and fat-reducing botanical and is the source of hydroxycitric acid (HCA).

qa.q: How does Garcinia cambogia HCA help with weight?
qa.a: HCA inhibits the enzyme ATP citrate lyase, suppressing lipogenesis (conversion of carbohydrates into fat) and enhancing fat oxidation, while raising serotonin to reduce appetite and emotional eating. Trials show modest weight loss and reduced BMI at HCA doses of 1500–2800 mg/day.

qa.q: What is garcinol?
qa.a: Garcinol is a constituent of the Garcinia cambogia rind with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, shown to inhibit COX-2 and protect tissues from oxidative stress in laboratory studies.

qa.q: Who should avoid Garcinia cambogia?
qa.a: It should be avoided during pregnancy and lactation, used cautiously by diabetics (may lower blood sugar), by people on SSRIs or mood stabilizers (HCA increases serotonin), and by those with liver disorders — only under supervision, especially with high-concentration extracts.

entries.title: Vrikshamla (Garcinia cambogia)
entries.summary: Monograph for Vrikshamla / Malabar Tamarind (Garcinia cambogia), a Guttiferae fruit whose HCA-rich rind is a non-stimulant aid for weight management, appetite control, lipid balance and digestion; GreenSpace Herbs supplies it standardized to 50–60% HCA with cited clinical references.
facts: Vrikshamla (Garcinia cambogia), the Malabar Tamarind, is an evergreen Guttiferae (Clusiaceae) tree whose fruit rind (pericarp) is the primary medicinal and culinary part.
In Ayurveda it is classed as Amla (sour) and Laghu (light), used as a digestive, carminative and fat-reducing agent.
Hydroxycitric acid (HCA) is the primary active, present at up to 30% in the dried rind; other constituents include citric acid, polyphenols, flavonoids and garcinol.
HCA inhibits ATP citrate lyase to suppress lipogenesis and increases satiety by raising serotonin levels in the brain.
It is described as a non-stimulant, plant-based aid for weight management, appetite control and metabolic balance.

Randomized controlled trials demonstrated modest weight loss and reduced BMI with Garcinia extract at HCA 1500–2800 mg/day; HCA supplementation reduced serum triglyceride and LDL levels in overweight subjects.
Extracts are standardized to 50–60% HCA; forms include dried rind, capsules, tablets, teas and syrups, often combined with Triphala or Guggulu.
Grows across the Western Ghats of India (Kerala, Karnataka, Maharashtra), Indonesia, Thailand, Myanmar, Sri Lanka and parts of Central and West Africa.
Cited references include Onakpoya et al. (Journal of Obesity, 2011) systematic review/meta-analysis and Hayamizu et al. (Journal of Oleo Science, 2003) on serum lipids and visceral fat.

qa.q: Is Vrikshamla a stimulant?
qa.a: No. Vrikshamla (Garcinia cambogia / Malabar Tamarind) is described as a non-stimulant, plant-based aid for weight management, appetite control and metabolic balance. Its main active, HCA, works by inhibiting fat synthesis and increasing satiety via serotonin rather than stimulating the nervous system.

qa.q: What benefits does Garcinia cambogia offer beyond weight?
qa.a: Beyond modest weight and BMI reduction, it may reduce serum triglycerides, total cholesterol and LDL, improve glucose tolerance in insulin-resistant individuals, support digestion (stimulating enzymes and gastric emptying), and its garcinol and polyphenols protect the liver and pancreas from oxidative stress.

qa.q: How is Vrikshamla taken?
qa.a: It comes as dried rind (whole or powdered) for culinary and herbal use, and as extracts standardized to 50–60% HCA in capsules, tablets, teas and syrups — often combined with Triphala or Guggulu. It is most effective alongside a balanced diet, mindful eating and physical activity.

entries.title: Mandukaparni / Gotu Kola (Centella asiatica Linn.)
entries.summary: Botanical extract monograph for Centella asiatica (Mandukaparni / Gotu Kola), a creeping perennial of the Apiaceae family standardized to triterpenoid saponins (Asiaticoside, Madecassoside) and offered by GreenSpace Herbs as a standardized botanical extract for B2B supply.
facts: Centella asiatica Linn., known in Ayurveda as Mandukaparni and widely as Gotu Kola, is a prostrate, stoloniferous perennial herb of the family Apiaceae.
Its chief chemical constituents are triterpenoid and saponin glycosides, principally Asiaticoside and Madecassoside, together with Asiatic acid.
The whole aerial herb (creeping stems together with the fan-shaped leaves) is the part used and is the source of the standardized extract.
It is native to India, China, Indonesia, Australia, the South Pacific, Madagascar, and Southern and Middle Africa, favouring marshy areas and river banks in warm tropical to subtropical climates.

In Ayurveda it is classed among the medhya (intellect/nervous-system) herbs and is described as cooling and calming — Rasa: Tikta (bitter), Kashaya (astringent); Virya: Sheeta (cooling); Vipaka: Madhura (sweet); balances all three doshas.

Traditional pharmacological uses include treatment of leprosy and skin disorders such as psoriasis and eczema, memory support, respiratory ailments and strengthening of veins including varicose veins.
Nutraceutical roles include blood purifier, stress relief and immune booster.
It is available from GreenSpace Herbs as a standardized botanical extract for B2B supply.

qa.q: What is Mandukaparni standardized to?
qa.a: GreenSpace Herbs' Mandukaparni (Centella asiatica) extract is standardized to its triterpenoid saponins, chiefly Asiaticoside and Madecassoside, along with Asiatic acid — the marker compounds used to identify and quality-assure the botanical.

qa.q: What is Centella asiatica used for?
qa.a: Traditionally it is used for skin disorders (psoriasis, eczema, leprosy), memory support, respiratory ailments, and to strengthen veins including in varicose veins. As a nutraceutical it is valued as a blood purifier, stress reliever and immune booster.

qa.q: What plant family and part is used?
qa.a: Centella asiatica belongs to the family Apiaceae, and the whole aerial herb — creeping stems together with the fan-shaped leaves — is the part used for the extract.

entries.title: Mandukaparni / Gotu Kola (Centella asiatica) — Medhya Rasayana
entries.summary: Detailed monograph for Centella asiatica (Mandukaparni / Gotu Kola / Indian Pennywort), a Medhya Rasayana used for cognition, nerve calming, wound healing and circulation, standardized to asiaticoside/madecassoside and available from GreenSpace Herbs as a B2B botanical extract, with research highlights and safety notes.
facts: Centella asiatica (Mandukaparni / Gotu Kola / Indian Pennywort) is a Medhya Rasayana revered in Ayurveda, Siddha and Traditional Chinese Medicine for enhancing cognition, calming the nervous system and accelerating wound healing.
The Sanskrit name Mandukaparni means 'frog-leafed', referencing its fan-shaped leaves that resemble a frog's foot.
Latin name is Centella asiatica (L.) Urban; family Apiaceae (formerly Umbelliferae); it balances all three doshas — Vata, Pitta and Kapha.
Key active compounds: triterpenoid saponins (asiaticoside, madecassoside, asiatic acid), flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol), plus volatile oils, sterols, tannins and amino acids.
The whole aerial plant (leaves and stems) is used; triterpenoid glycosides asiaticoside and madecassoside are primarily responsible for wound-healing and collagen-boosting properties.

Benefits include enhanced memory/concentration, reduced anxiety and mental fatigue, neuroprotection, accelerated collagen synthesis and skin repair, reduced stretch marks/keloids/psoriatic plaques, and support in chronic venous insufficiency, varicose veins and hemorrhoids.
It features in classical formulations such as Brahmi Ghrita and Saraswatarishta.

Research highlights: clinical trials show improved working memory, focus and mood; topical use accelerates epithelialization and collagen deposition; Centella extract improves microcirculation and reduces edema in venous insufficiency.

Safety cautions: use under medical supervision in pregnancy/lactation; rare hepatotoxic reactions with long-term high-dose use; may enhance CNS depressants; discontinue at least 2 weeks before surgery due to potential blood sugar effects.
Available from GreenSpace Herbs as a standardized triterpenoid extract (asiaticoside/madecassoside) for B2B supply.

qa.q: What does Gotu Kola (Mandukaparni) do for the brain?
qa.a: As a Medhya Rasayana it enhances memory, concentration and mental clarity, reduces anxiety and mental fatigue, and acts as a neuroprotective agent in cognitive decline and stress-induced brain aging; clinical trials show improvements in working memory, focus and mood.

qa.q: Is Centella asiatica good for skin and wound healing?
qa.a: Yes. Its triterpenoid glycosides accelerate collagen synthesis and skin repair, aid epithelialization and scar remodeling, reduce stretch marks, keloids and psoriatic plaques, and improve microcirculation — which is why it is widely used in cosmeceutical and anti-aging formulations.

qa.q: Are there any precautions with Mandukaparni?
qa.a: It is generally gentle but should be used under medical supervision in pregnancy and lactation; rare hepatotoxic reactions have been reported with long-term high-dose use; it may enhance sedatives/CNS depressants; and it should be discontinued at least 2 weeks before scheduled surgery.

qa.q: What is Mandukaparni standardized to?
qa.a: GreenSpace Herbs offers it as a standardized triterpenoid extract, standardized to asiaticoside and madecassoside content.

entries.title: Cocrystal Engineering: Unlocking the Bioavailability Potential of Poorly Soluble Nutraceuticals
entries.summary: A GreenSpace Herbs science blog article (Dec 30, 2025, by Dr. Anand Solomon, Principal Scientist) explaining how cocrystal engineering lowers lattice energy to dramatically improve the aqueous solubility and bioavailability of stubborn, poorly soluble nutraceuticals like quercetin, resveratrol and boswellic acids.
facts: The article was published December 30, 2025 on the GreenSpace Herbs blog and authored by Dr. Anand Solomon, Principal Scientist.
Roughly 70% of newly discovered phytochemicals fail to clear the first hurdle of aqueous solubility, creating a bottleneck between benchtop and patient.

Poorly soluble molecules like quercetin, resveratrol and boswellic acids pack into highly ordered arrays held by dense hydrogen bonding and π–π stacking, producing high lattice energy that resists dissolution.
Cocrystals introduce a secondary molecule (a coformer) into the crystal lattice to disrupt cohesive packing, lowering the activation energy for dissolution while retaining crystalline stability.

The 'Spring and Parachute' mechanism: the hydrophilic coformer dissociates rapidly (spring) to create supersaturation, then inhibits nucleation of the parent drug (parachute) to sustain high concentration long enough for absorption.

Reported solubility gains include itraconazole up nearly 3,000% with L-tartaric acid, near-total gliclazide–tromethamine dissolution within 10 minutes (vs ~10% for pure API), and a mirtazapine–oxalate salt cocrystal achieving a 13,650% solubility enhancement.

Scalable cocrystal synthesis uses mechanochemical methods such as Liquid-Assisted Grinding (LAG), electrospraying and resonant acoustic mixing, moving from milligrams to kilograms with minimal solvent waste.
Modern computational tools predict 'supramolecular synthons', anticipating how coformers like citric acid or L-proline dock with a target molecule.

qa.q: What is cocrystal engineering and why does it matter for nutraceuticals?
qa.a: Cocrystal engineering introduces a secondary 'coformer' molecule into a compound's crystal lattice to disrupt its tight packing and lower the energy needed to dissolve — dramatically boosting the aqueous solubility and bioavailability of poorly soluble bioactives, without altering the molecule's chemistry or sacrificing crystalline stability.

qa.q: What is the 'Spring and Parachute' effect?
qa.a: When a cocrystal hits water, the hydrophilic coformer dissociates quickly (the 'spring'), catapulting the drug into supersaturation far above equilibrium; the coformer then inhibits the parent drug from precipitating (the 'parachute'), sustaining that high concentration long enough for the body to absorb it.

qa.q: How big can cocrystal solubility improvements be?
qa.a: Gains can be enormous — the article cites itraconazole solubility rising nearly 3,000% with L-tartaric acid and an outlier mirtazapine–oxalate salt cocrystal achieving a 13,650% enhancement, alongside gliclazide–tromethamine reaching near-total dissolution in 10 minutes versus about 10% for the pure ingredient.

entries.title: Indian Coleus (Coleus forskohlii)
entries.summary: Detailed monograph for Coleus forskohlii (Makandi / Pashanabhedi / Indian Coleus), a Lamiaceae herb whose tuberous roots yield forskolin, standardized to 10–20% forskolin and used for weight/metabolic, cardiovascular, respiratory, thyroid, glucose and eye-health support; available from GreenSpace Herbs as a B2B botanical extract.
facts: Coleus forskohlii (also Pashanabhedi, Makandi or Indian Coleus) is a perennial aromatic herb of the Lamiaceae family with fleshy tuberous roots; the roots are the primary medicinal part.
GreenSpace Herbs offers it as a standardized botanical extract containing 10–20% forskolin.

Forskolin is a labdane diterpenoid that directly activates adenylate cyclase, raising intracellular cAMP — a signalling molecule governing fat breakdown, vascular tone, bronchial relaxation and hormone release; other diterpenes include dehydroxyforskolin and coleonol.
In Ayurveda it is known as Pashanabhedi ('breaks stones') and regulates the Vata and Kapha doshas, used traditionally as a cardiovascular and digestive tonic.
It is native to South Asia, growing across India (especially the Deccan Plateau and South India), Nepal, Sri Lanka and Thailand.

Benefits include increased lipolysis for weight/metabolic support, vasodilation that lowers systolic and diastolic blood pressure without affecting heart rate, bronchial relaxation for asthma/bronchitis, stimulation of thyroid hormones T3 and T4, enhanced insulin sensitivity, and reduced intraocular pressure for glaucoma.

Research highlights: forskolin extract reduced body fat percentage and improved lean mass in overweight men; reduced blood pressure without reflex tachycardia; inhaled forskolin improved FEV1 in asthmatics; increased T3 and T4 without affecting TSH.

Precautions: avoid in pregnancy/lactation (potential uterine stimulant), caution in hypotension, may potentiate calcium channel blockers or beta-blockers, and may enhance bleeding risk (avoid before surgery).

qa.q: What is Coleus forskohlii standardized to and what is its active compound?
qa.a: GreenSpace Herbs' Coleus forskohlii extract is standardized to 10–20% forskolin, a labdane diterpenoid from the tuberous root that directly activates adenylate cyclase to raise intracellular cAMP.

qa.q: How does forskolin help with weight loss?
qa.a: Forskolin raises cellular cAMP, which promotes fat breakdown (lipolysis). Clinical studies of forskolin extract in overweight and obese men showed reduced body-fat percentage and improved lean mass, making it a common ingredient in herbal weight-management formulations.

qa.q: What are Coleus forskohlii's other benefits?
qa.a: Beyond metabolism it acts as a vasodilator that lowers blood pressure without raising heart rate, relaxes bronchial muscle to support asthma and bronchitis, stimulates thyroid hormones T3 and T4, enhances insulin sensitivity, and reduces intraocular pressure in glaucoma.

qa.q: Who should avoid Coleus forskohlii?
qa.a: It should be avoided in pregnancy and lactation (potential uterine stimulant), used cautiously in people with low blood pressure, and avoided before surgery due to bleeding risk; it may also potentiate calcium channel blockers or beta-blockers, so use it under supervision if on heart medication.

entries.title: Coleus (Coleus forskohlii Briq.)
entries.summary: Botanical extract monograph for Coleus forskohlii Briq. (family Labiatae/Lamiaceae), an aromatic perennial whose tuberous root is the only known natural source of the diterpene forskohlin; standardized to forskohlin and offered by GreenSpace Herbs for B2B supply, with traditional cardiovascular, dermatological and respiratory uses.
facts: Coleus forskohlii Briq. is a small, branched, aromatic perennial herb of the family Labiatae (mint family, Lamiaceae); the root is the part used.

It is the only known natural source of forskohlin (also written forskolin), a labdane diterpene concentrated in the tuberous root and used as the marker compound for identification and standardization.
It is distributed across the subtropical Himalayas of Kumaon and Nepal, ascending to about 8,000 ft, and southward through the Deccan peninsula, Gujarat and Bihar.
Ayurvedic profile — Rasa: Katu (pungent), Tikta (bitter); Virya: Ushna (heating); Vipaka: Katu (pungent); pacifies Kapha and Vata in keeping with its warming, aromatic nature.
Cardiovascular uses: forskohlin is traditionally indicated in hypertension, congestive heart failure and angina pectoris — the uses for which the botanical is best known.
Dermatological and respiratory uses: also indicated in eczema (atopic dermatitis), psoriasis, asthma and glaucoma.
Available from GreenSpace Herbs as a standardized botanical extract for B2B supply.

qa.q: What compound makes Coleus forskohlii unique?
qa.a: Coleus forskohlii Briq. is the only known natural source of forskohlin (forskolin), a labdane diterpene found in its tuberous root, which is the marker compound used to standardize and quality-assure the extract.

qa.q: What is Coleus (Coleus forskohlii) used for?
qa.a: It is best known for cardiovascular uses — hypertension, congestive heart failure and angina pectoris — and is also indicated in dermatological and respiratory conditions including eczema, psoriasis, asthma and glaucoma.

qa.q: Where does Coleus forskohlii grow?
qa.a: It grows across the subtropical Himalayas of Kumaon and Nepal up to about 8,000 ft and southward through the Deccan peninsula, Gujarat and Bihar, favouring well-drained, sun-exposed slopes and seasonally dry terrain.

entries.title: Salaiguggul / Boswellia (Boswellia serrata Roxb.)
entries.summary: Monograph for Boswellia serrata (Salaiguggul / Shallaki / Indian Frankincense), a Burseraceae oleo-gum-resin standardized to boswellic acids (AKBA) and supplied by GreenSpace Herbs as an anti-inflammatory, joint-health botanical extract for B2B.
facts: Boswellia serrata (Salaiguggul / Shallaki / Indian Frankincense) is a Burseraceae tree whose incised bark yields a golden-yellow oleo-gum-resin used as the primary medicinal part.
Its key actives are boswellic acids — alpha-boswellic acid, beta-boswellic acid, and acetyl-11-keto-beta-boswellic acid (AKBA); the Quick Facts list standardization to Boswellic Acids (AKBA).
AKBA is the most potent anti-inflammatory agent in Boswellia, inhibiting 5-lipoxygenase (5-LOX), a key enzyme in the inflammation pathway.
In Ayurveda it is classified under Vedanasthapana (pain-relieving) and Sandhivatahara (joint disorder-relieving) categories and indicated in Vata-Pitta conditions.
It is grown in India (Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, Rajasthan, Maharashtra), Northern Africa, the Middle East and Pakistan, in arid to semi-arid dry deciduous forests.
A randomized double-blind placebo-controlled trial (Kimmatkar et al., 2003, Phytomedicine) reported significant improvement in pain and function in osteoarthritis of the knee.
Boswellic acids were shown to suppress human leukemia HL-60 cell growth by inducing apoptosis (Hostanska et al., 2002).
Caution is advised in pregnancy/lactation, autoimmune disease (may stimulate immune function), and before surgery or in bleeding disorders (mild anticoagulant effect).

qa.q: What does Boswellia (Salaiguggul) do?
qa.a: Boswellia serrata is a natural anti-inflammatory and analgesic used for joint and musculoskeletal health (osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, back pain), digestive/IBD support (ulcerative colitis, Crohn's), and respiratory conditions like asthma and bronchitis. Its boswellic acids, especially AKBA, inhibit the 5-LOX inflammation enzyme, offering relief without the GI side effects of NSAIDs.

qa.q: What is Boswellia standardized to?
qa.a: GreenSpace Herbs supplies Boswellia serrata as a standardized botanical extract standardized to boswellic acids (AKBA), the most potent anti-inflammatory constituent. It is available for B2B supply — contact via the enquiry/Partner With Us link.

qa.q: Is Boswellia safe to take long-term?
qa.a: It is generally well tolerated and considered a safer alternative to NSAIDs and corticosteroids for long-term use, but high-dose or prolonged use should be supervised. Caution applies in pregnancy/lactation, autoimmune disease, and before surgery or with bleeding disorders due to a mild anticoagulant effect.

entries.title: Boswellia (Boswellia serrata) — Salaiguggul / Shallaki
entries.summary: Detailed Boswellia serrata monograph (Salaiguggul / Shallaki / Indian Frankincense, family Burseraceae) with a specified standardization of >=85% total boswellic acids and >=60% AKBA, positioned as a clinically researched anti-inflammatory joint-health extract for B2B supply.
facts: This Boswellia serrata extract is specified in Quick Facts as standardized to >=85% total boswellic acids and >=60% AKBA.
The medicinal part is the oleo-gum-resin (bark exudate); actives are boswellic acids (alpha, beta, and AKBA) plus essential oils (pinene) and polysaccharides/tannins.
AKBA selectively inhibits 5-lipoxygenase (5-LOX) in the leukotriene inflammation pathway; pharmacology also cites inhibition of pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-alpha, IL-1beta and IL-6.
It is referenced in classical Ayurvedic literature such as the Charaka Samhita and traditionally promotes Sandhigata Vata (joint health).

Ideal growing conditions are altitude 300–1200 metres, rocky/sandy well-drained soils, and arid to semi-arid climates; distribution spans India, North Africa, the Middle East, Pakistan and parts of Africa and the Americas.
Used for osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis, IBD (ulcerative colitis and Crohn's), asthma/bronchitis, and chronic pain; often combined with Guggulu, Ashwagandha and Turmeric.

Precautions: caution in pregnancy/lactation, autoimmune disease, and before surgery/bleeding disorders; uncommon side effects include mild gastric discomfort, skin rash (topical) and rare headache or nausea.

qa.q: What is the standardization of GreenSpace's Boswellia extract?
qa.a: This page specifies the Boswellia serrata extract as standardized to at least 85% total boswellic acids and at least 60% AKBA (acetyl-11-keto-beta-boswellic acid), the most potent anti-inflammatory constituent.

qa.q: How does Boswellia reduce inflammation?
qa.a: Its boswellic acid AKBA selectively inhibits 5-lipoxygenase (5-LOX), a key enzyme in the leukotriene inflammation pathway, and downregulates pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-alpha, IL-1beta and IL-6 — supporting use in arthritis, inflammatory bowel disease and respiratory inflammation without the gastric burden of NSAIDs.

qa.q: What is Boswellia used for in formulations?
qa.a: It features in joint-health and mobility supplements, inflammation and metabolic-health formulas, fortified wellness teas/beverages, and pain-relief gummies and bars — typically formulated alongside Ashwagandha, Guggulu or Turmeric.

entries.title: Bromelain (Ananas comosus)
entries.summary: Monograph for Bromelain, a proteolytic cysteine-protease enzyme complex extracted from pineapple (Ananas comosus, family Bromeliaceae), supplied by GreenSpace Herbs as a standardized enzyme extract for systemic enzyme therapy, anti-inflammatory action, edema reduction and muscle recovery.
facts: Bromelain is a proteolytic enzyme complex (a cysteine protease / thiol endopeptidase) extracted from the stem and fruit of the pineapple plant, Ananas comosus (family Bromeliaceae).
It is supplied as a standardized enzyme extract measured by proteolytic activity in GDU / FIP units.
Extraction uses aqueous extraction followed by ultrafiltration or freeze-drying, sourced from South America and tropical Asia (India, Philippines, Thailand).
Mechanistically it inhibits NF-kB activation, downregulates iNOS and COX-2, and suppresses MAPK signalling (ERK, JNK, p38), reducing TNF-alpha, IL-6 and nitric oxide.
Suggested dose is 500–2000 mg/day of standardized bromelain extract; it is frequently combined with Curcuma longa, Zingiber officinale, Carica papaya (Papain), Withania somnifera and Triphala.
Documented benefits include reduced post-surgical pain/swelling/bruising (oral surgery, sports injuries, sinusitis) and minimized exercise-induced muscle damage markers (CK, LDH).
The extract is Non-GMO, Vegan, Gluten-Free, third-party tested for purity and proteolytic activity, with Kosher and Halal available; it is derived sustainably from pineapple stem-waste by-products.

Cited research includes Bottega et al. 2021, Colletti et al. 2024 (third-molar swelling), Chakraborty et al. 2021, Tsai et al. 2023 (colorectal cancer chemotherapy synergy) and Shing et al. 2016 (multi-day cycling muscle recovery).

qa.q: What is bromelain and where does it come from?
qa.a: Bromelain is a proteolytic (protein-digesting) cysteine-protease enzyme complex extracted from the stem and fruit of the pineapple plant, Ananas comosus. It is used in systemic enzyme therapy for protein digestion, anti-inflammatory action, edema and pain reduction, and muscle recovery.

qa.q: How is bromelain standardized and dosed?
qa.a: It is supplied as a standardized enzyme extract measured by proteolytic activity in GDU / FIP units. A typical suggested dose is 500–2000 mg/day of standardized bromelain extract, and it pairs well with turmeric, ginger, papain (papaya) and ashwagandha in enzyme blends.

qa.q: Is bromelain clinically studied and safe?
qa.a: Yes — a growing evidence base supports its role in enzyme therapy across inflammatory, post-surgical, muscle-recovery and oncology-adjunct research (e.g. Colletti 2024, Tsai 2023, Shing 2016). It is well tolerated at recommended doses, and GreenSpace's extract is third-party tested and clean-label (Non-GMO, Vegan, Gluten-Free, Kosher/Halal available).

entries.title: Green Tea (Camellia sinensis Kuntze)
entries.summary: In-depth Green Tea monograph (Camellia sinensis, family Theaceae) covering its catechin/EGCG and L-theanine chemistry and antioxidant, metabolic, cognitive and cardiovascular benefits, supplied by GreenSpace Herbs as a standardized botanical extract for B2B nutraceutical use.
facts: Green tea is made from the unoxidized (minimally processed) young leaves and buds of Camellia sinensis, an evergreen Theaceae shrub, retaining high catechin antioxidant levels.
Key phytochemicals are catechins (EGCG, EGC, ECG, EC), theanine (a calming amino acid), caffeine, flavonoids/tannins, and vitamins C and B complex plus minerals (zinc, selenium).
EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate) is the most researched catechin, with effects on metabolism, inflammation and cellular protection; the extract is standardized to catechins / EGCG.
In Ayurveda green tea is Pitta-pacifying (cooling, bitter, astringent) and is valued within Medhya Rasayana traditions for cognitive vitality.
It is cultivated in over 30 countries, notably India, China, Myanmar, Japan and Korea, favouring cooler misty highlands and slightly acidic, well-drained loamy soils.
A 2020 meta-analysis found green tea intake significantly reduces blood pressure and LDL cholesterol; clinical trials show enhanced fat metabolism and improved BMI in overweight adults.
The L-theanine and caffeine combination improves focus without overstimulation and supports attention and working memory in human studies.
Green tea is recognized by EFSA, NIH and WHO for its functional food and nutraceutical value.
Precautions: limit to 1–2 cups/day in pregnancy/breastfeeding (caffeine); tannins reduce iron absorption; excessive extract intake has been associated with liver toxicity.

qa.q: What are the health benefits of green tea?
qa.a: Green tea (Camellia sinensis) provides antioxidant and anti-inflammatory protection, cognitive and neuroprotective support via L-theanine and caffeine, cardiovascular benefits (lower LDL cholesterol and blood pressure), and metabolic/weight-management support with improved insulin sensitivity. EGCG, its main catechin, is also studied for anti-proliferative activity in preclinical cancer research.

qa.q: What is EGCG in green tea?
qa.a: EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate) is the most researched catechin in green tea, a powerful polyphenolic antioxidant with documented effects on metabolism, inflammation and cellular protection. GreenSpace's green tea extract is standardized to its catechin/EGCG content.

qa.q: Is green tea safe to drink daily?
qa.a: Yes, in moderation. Cautions include limiting to 1–2 cups per day during pregnancy/breastfeeding due to caffeine, avoiding it with meals if iron-deficient (tannins reduce iron absorption), and noting that excessive concentrated-extract intake has been associated with liver toxicity.

qa.a: GreenSpace supplies Camellia sinensis (green tea) as a standardized botanical extract standardized to catechins, led by EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate), for use in functional foods, weight-management and metabolism formulas, sports/focus supplements and cosmetic actives.

qa.q: Does green tea interact with medications?
qa.a: It may interact with blood thinners, stimulants and beta-blockers, and its caffeine can cause insomnia or palpitations in sensitive people. Its tannins can also reduce iron absorption, so it should be avoided with meals by those with iron-deficiency anemia.

qa.q: Where is green tea grown?
qa.a: Green tea is cultivated in over 30 countries, with traditional heartlands in South and Southeast Asia (India, China, Myanmar), refined steamed-leaf and matcha traditions in Japan, and prized highland gardens in Korea. Cooler, misty highlands yield higher-quality, catechin-rich leaves.

entries.title: Vasaka (Adhatoda vasica Nees)
entries.summary: A botanical monograph for Vasaka (Adhatoda vasica Nees), an Acanthaceae respiratory herb known as 'the lungs' natural guardian.' It details the plant, its vasicine and vasicinone actives, expectorant/bronchodilator benefits, forms of use, research and safety, offered by GreenSpace as a standardized extract. Appears to be a duplicate/copy-test version of the Vasaka page.
facts: Vasaka (Adhatoda vasica Nees, also referenced as Justicia adhatoda) is a tall evergreen aromatic shrub of the family Acanthaceae, native to the Indian subcontinent.
In Ayurveda it is classified as a Pittashamaka (cooling/soothing) herb used to clear respiratory congestion, break down phlegm and soothe inflamed mucous membranes.

Key active phytochemicals are Vasicine (a bronchodilator and expectorant) and Vasicinone (formed by oxidation of vasicine); actions include anti-inflammatory, antitussive, mucolytic and antimicrobial.
Leaves are the principal part used; flowers, roots and bark are also medicinally valuable, and it is standardized to vasicine.
It features in classical formulations such as Vasavaleha, Sitopaladi churna and Talisadi churna, and was historically used as an adjunct for pulmonary tuberculosis.

Research notes: vasicine's bronchodilatory action is comparable to theophylline in lab studies; aqueous extracts show antitussive activity in animal models; and it is antibacterial against respiratory pathogens such as Streptococcus pneumoniae.

Safety: avoid in pregnancy (vasicine may stimulate uterine contractions); use caution with anticoagulants or bronchodilators; safe for children only in diluted, age-appropriate doses under professional advice.
Distribution spans India, Nepal, Sri Lanka, Pakistan and Bangladesh, plus parts of Southeast Asia and East Africa.

qa.q: What does Vasaka do?
qa.a: Vasaka (Adhatoda vasica) is a respiratory herb that acts as an expectorant and bronchodilator, loosening mucus, easing breathlessness, and soothing inflamed airways. It is traditionally used for cough, bronchitis and asthma, driven by its alkaloid vasicine.

qa.q: What are the active compounds in Vasaka?
qa.a: The chief actives are vasicine (a bronchodilator and expectorant) and vasicinone (formed by oxidation of vasicine). The GreenSpace extract is standardized to vasicine content.

qa.q: Is Vasaka safe during pregnancy?
qa.a: No — Vasaka should be avoided in pregnancy, particularly early stages, because vasicine may stimulate uterine contractions. Caution is also advised alongside anticoagulants or bronchodilators.

facts: Vasaka is an evergreen, gregarious, perennial herb of the Acanthaceae family, botanically identified as Adhatoda vasica Nees.
It is distributed across the plains of India and into the lower Himalayan ranges, forming dense natural stands in the wild.
The leaves, flowers, fruits and roots are all used medicinally, most prominently for cough, cold, chronic bronchitis and asthma.
Its principal constituents are alkaloids, the chief alkaloid being vasicine — the marker compound to which the standardized extract is calibrated.
Vasaka acts as an expectorant (loosening and mobilising respiratory secretions) and an antispasmodic (easing airway spasm of irritable coughs).
The light-green leaves have a characteristic odour and a distinctly bitter taste, traditional indicators of alkaloid-rich material.
It is available from GreenSpace as a standardized leaf extract calibrated to vasicine for B2B supply.

qa.q: What is Vasaka used for?
qa.a: Vasaka (Adhatoda vasica) is a respiratory herb used traditionally for cough, cold, chronic bronchitis and asthma. It works as an expectorant to clear respiratory secretions and as an antispasmodic to ease airway spasm.

qa.q: How is the Vasaka extract standardized?
qa.a: GreenSpace's Vasaka is a standardized leaf extract calibrated to its chief alkaloid, vasicine, which is the marker compound ensuring consistent dosing.

qa.q: Where does Vasaka grow?
qa.a: It is native to the Indian subcontinent, growing gregariously throughout the plains of India and ascending into the lower Himalayan foothills as a hardy evergreen perennial.

entries.title: Kalmegh (Andrographis paniculata)
entries.summary: A botanical monograph for Kalmegh (Andrographis paniculata), the Acanthaceae 'King of Bitters' standardized to andrographolide. It covers the plant, its hepatoprotective, immune-modulating, antipyretic and digestive uses, forms, safety and research, offered by GreenSpace as a standardized extract for liver, immune and digestive formulations.
facts: Kalmegh (Andrographis paniculata (Burm.f.) Wall. ex Nees) is a fast-growing erect annual herb of the family Acanthaceae, known as the 'King of Bitters'; its Sanskrit name means 'dark cloud.'
In Ayurveda it is a Tikta Rasayana (bitter rejuvenator) used mainly to balance Pitta and Kapha doshas.
The leaves and aerial parts are the medicinally active material; the standardized extract is calibrated to 10% Andrographolides.
Key phytochemicals include andrographolide (the primary bioactive), neoandrographolide, flavonoids, xanthones and diterpene lactones.
Andrographolide has anti-inflammatory, antiviral, hepatoprotective and immunostimulant properties, protecting liver cells against paracetamol-induced toxicity in models.

Traditional uses include liver/hepatoprotective support (jaundice, fatty liver, hepatitis), immune modulation and fever (dengue, malaria, viral fevers, upper respiratory infections), and digestive/antimicrobial action.
It is used in classical formulations such as Bhumi Amalaki Churna and Arogyavardhini Vati.

Precautions: avoid in pregnancy/lactation (may stimulate uterine contractions), use caution in autoimmune disorders and with immunosuppressants or anticoagulants; high doses may cause gastric irritation, appetite loss or allergic rash.
It is native to India and Sri Lanka and cultivated across tropical/subtropical regions, preferring 1,000–1,500 mm annual rainfall.

qa.q: What is Kalmegh good for?
qa.a: Kalmegh (Andrographis paniculata) is known as the 'King of Bitters' and is used mainly for liver/hepatoprotective support, immune modulation, fever reduction and digestion. Its primary bioactive, andrographolide, has anti-inflammatory, antiviral, hepatoprotective and immunostimulant properties.

qa.q: How is the Kalmegh extract standardized?
qa.a: GreenSpace's Kalmegh is a standardized extract calibrated to 10% andrographolides, the primary bioactive compound derived from the leaves and aerial parts.

qa.q: Who should avoid Kalmegh?
qa.a: It should be avoided in pregnancy and lactation and used only under professional guidance in children under five. Caution is advised for people with autoimmune disorders or those taking immunosuppressants or anticoagulants, and high doses may cause gastric irritation or allergic rash.

entries.title: Ayurveda Science — The Science of Life
entries.summary: A pillar research page explaining Ayurveda's core framework (doshas, five elements, prevention) and how GreenSpace Herbs re-reads that 5,000-year-old system through modern analytical chemistry and quantum science to develop evidence-led botanical actives.
facts: Ayurveda originated in India over 5,000 years ago; the name combines Sanskrit 'Ayur' (life) and 'Veda' (science), meaning the 'Science of Life.'
The five elements (Pancha Mahabhutas) are Earth (Prithvi), Fire (Agni), Air (Vayu), Water (Jal) and Space (Akasha).
The three doshas are Vata (Air + Ether, governing movement), Pitta (Fire + Water, governing digestion/transformation) and Kapha (Earth + Water, governing structure/stability).
A person's unique combination of doshas is called their Prakriti — their innate bodily constitution.

Acharya Sushruta defined health as balance among the doshas, proper digestion (Agni), well-nourished tissues (Dhatus) and efficient elimination of waste (Malas), plus clarity of soul, senses and mind.
Charaka's dictum: 'Rogastu Dosha Vaishamyam, Dosha Samyam Arogata' — disease occurs when doshas are imbalanced; health is restored when they are balanced.
Prevention is emphasized through Dinacharya (daily routines), Ritucharya (seasonal routines), proper diet, and lifestyle/mind practices.
Sage Bharadwaja (circa 700 BC) is regarded as the father of Ayurveda for the human race; the science is organized into eight specialized branches.
Rasayana (rejuvenative/longevity/geriatric therapy) and Vajikarana (reproduction and vital strength) are the branches most resonant with GreenSpace's work.
The page draws conceptual parallels between Ayurveda and quantum science: Prabhava (effect) parallels non-intuitive quantum effects, and Ojas (vital energy) resembles quantum energy states.

GreenSpace's quantum herbal formulation approach uses quantum molecular modelling (NMR-based prediction), synergy prediction, study of Bhasmas (nano-preparations of metals/minerals), and quantum-dot targeted delivery inspired by marma points.

The page candidly notes challenges: limited rigorous peer-reviewed evidence, cautious reception over metaphysical claims, and variable standardization — insisting metaphor be kept separate from verified science.

qa.q: What does 'Ayurveda' mean?
qa.a: Ayurveda comes from the Sanskrit words 'Ayur' (life) and 'Veda' (science), so it literally means the 'Science of Life.' It is a holistic Indian system of medicine over 5,000 years old that emphasizes balance of body, mind and spirit to promote health and prevent disease.

qa.q: What are the three doshas?
qa.a: The three doshas are Vata (Air + Ether, governing movement and communication), Pitta (Fire + Water, governing digestion and transformation) and Kapha (Earth + Water, governing structure and stability). Everyone carries a unique combination, called their Prakriti.

qa.q: How does GreenSpace Herbs connect Ayurveda with quantum science?
qa.a: GreenSpace reads Ayurvedic principles through analytical chemistry, pharmacokinetics and quantum modelling. It explores parallels like Prabhava (effect) with quantum non-locality and Ojas (vital energy) with quantum energy states, while insisting every claim be grounded in evidence and that philosophy be kept distinct from verified science.

qa.q: What are the eight branches of Ayurveda relevant to GreenSpace?
qa.a: Sage Bharadwaja organized Ayurveda into eight interconnected branches. The two most relevant to GreenSpace's work are Rasayana (rejuvenative and longevity therapies) and Vajikarana (reproduction and vital strength).

entries.title: Ayurveda & Supplements: The Science-Backed Ancient Code for Biohacking Your Health
entries.summary: A Gen Z-styled blog article framing Ayurveda as a 5,000-year-old personalized-health/biohacking system, explaining the three doshas and dosha-specific supplement strategies, and arguing bio-individuality is the future of the supplement industry.
facts: Ayurveda is described as a 5,000-year-old, science-backed system that predicted biohacking and personalized health.
The three doshas per the article: Pitta (Fire + Water) the intense go-getter type, Vata (Air + Space) the creative free-spirited type, and Kapha (Earth + Water) the chill nurturing type.
Dosha imbalances map to symptoms: excess Pitta to inflammation and skin breakouts, Vata overload to anxiety and digestive issues, excess Kapha to sluggishness and water retention.
Pitta-balancing supplements use cooling herbs like Amla, Shatavari and Licorice.
Vata-grounding supplements use adaptogens like Ashwagandha, Brahmi and ghee-based formulations for focus and reduced anxiety.
Kapha-energizing supplements use Trikatu (ginger, black pepper, long pepper), Turmeric and Guggul to boost metabolism.
Highlighted 'super botanicals' include Moringa (immunity, metabolism, vitality) and Nigella Sativa / Black Seed (anti-inflammatory, immune-boosting, brain-enhancing).
The article cites modern research support: Ashwagandha for cortisol control, Turmeric for inflammation, and Brahmi for brain function.
It argues the supplement industry is shifting from generic multivitamins toward bio-individuality — dosha-specific protein powders, adaptogenic blends and Ayurvedic nootropics.

qa.q: How do I choose supplements for my dosha?
qa.a: The article suggests matching supplements to your dominant dosha: cooling herbs like Amla, Shatavari and Licorice for Pitta; grounding adaptogens like Ashwagandha and Brahmi for Vata; and energizing ingredients like Trikatu, Turmeric and Guggul for Kapha. The key is keeping your doshas balanced.

qa.q: Is Ayurveda actually backed by science?
qa.a: The article points to modern research showing Ayurvedic herbs deliver measurable effects — Ashwagandha helps with cortisol control, Turmeric fights inflammation, and Brahmi enhances brain function — arguing that ancient wisdom is increasingly validated by studies.

qa.q: What is bio-individuality and why does it matter for supplements?
qa.a: Bio-individuality means supplements tailored to your unique body type and lifestyle rather than one-size-fits-all vitamins. The article frames Ayurveda as having practiced this for millennia and calls it the future of the supplement industry.

entries.title: Neem (Azadirachta indica Juss.)
entries.summary: Botanical extract monograph for Neem leaf, a bitter evergreen Indian tree of family Meliaceae standardized on nimbin, quercetin and ß-sitosterol, with reported anti-diabetic, antimicrobial and hepatoprotective activity — offered as a standardized B2B extract.
facts: Neem (Azadirachta indica A. Juss.) belongs to family Meliaceae and is a large evergreen tree found throughout the greater part of India.
The leaves contain chiefly nimbin (a triterpenoid/limonoid), quercetin (a flavonoid) and ß-sitosterol (a phytosterol).
The leaf is the principal part used for the standardized extract, though bark, seed and seed oil are also used traditionally.
Neem extracts have reported anti-diabetic, anti-bacterial and anti-viral properties.
Leaf extract fractions markedly delay blood clotting time and act as a mild antiseptic.
The aqueous leaf extract is hepatoprotective, decreases blood sugar, and prevents both adrenaline- and glucose-induced hyperglycaemia.
Clinical studies indicate neem leaf extract is effective in helping address ringworm, eczema and scabies.
The tree is drought-resistant, thrives in hot semi-arid to sub-humid conditions, and tolerates poor, marginal soils, making it a mainstay of Indian agroforestry.
Ayurvedic profile: Rasa (taste) Tikta (bitter) and Kashaya (astringent); Virya (potency) Shita (cooling); Vipaka (post-digestive) Katu (pungent); pacifies Kapha and Pitta.
Available as a standardized botanical extract for B2B supply.

qa.q: What are the active compounds in Neem leaf extract?
qa.a: Neem leaf contains chiefly nimbin (a triterpenoid/limonoid), quercetin (a flavonoid) and ß-sitosterol (a phytosterol). These underpin much of its documented biological activity.

qa.q: What is Neem used for?
qa.a: Neem leaf extract has reported anti-diabetic, anti-bacterial and anti-viral activity. It is hepatoprotective, helps lower blood sugar and prevent hyperglycaemia, delays blood clotting, acts as a mild antiseptic, and has clinical use against ringworm, eczema and scabies.

qa.q: What is Neem's Ayurvedic profile?
qa.a: In Ayurveda Neem (Nimba) is Tikta (bitter) and Kashaya (astringent) in taste, Shita (cooling) in potency, and Katu (pungent) in post-digestive effect. It pacifies the Kapha and Pitta doshas.

entries.title: Neem (Azadirachta indica)
entries.summary: Full botanical monograph for Neem, revered in Ayurveda as 'Sarva Roga Nivarini' (healer of all diseases) — a Meliaceae tree used whole (leaf, bark, seed/oil, flower) with azadirachtin and nimbidin, covering distribution, uses in skin/immunity/oral/gut health, research, and safety; offered as a standardized B2B extract.
facts: Neem is revered in Ayurveda as 'Sarva Roga Nivarini' — the healer of all diseases — and is classed as a natural antimicrobial, blood purifier (Rakta Shodhak) and detoxifier.
Botanically Azadirachta indica A. Juss., family Meliaceae; every part (leaves, bark, seeds, flowers, roots) is used therapeutically.
Ayurvedic profile: Tikta (bitter) and Kashaya (astringent) with Katu Vipaka (pungent post-digestive effect).

Key active compounds: Azadirachtin (signature limonoid, antiparasitic/anti-proliferative), Nimbidin (anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial), plus flavonoids, tannins and fatty acids concentrated in the oil.
Native to the Indian subcontinent (India, Nepal, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh) and naturalized in Africa, Southeast Asia, Australia, and parts of the Americas and Caribbean.

Therapeutic uses span skin and blood (acne, eczema, boils, fungal infections, detox), immunity/infection (kills lice, intestinal worms; disrupts Staphylococcus and E. coli), and oral/gut health (gum strength, plaque, gingivitis, colitis, NAFLD).
Neem sticks are traditionally used as toothbrushes; the plant features in krimighna (antiparasitic) and jwarahara (fever-reducing) formulations, often combined with Manjistha and Guduchi.

Research highlights: antibacterial against Staphylococcus aureus, Pseudomonas and E. coli; antifungal against Candida albicans and Aspergillus; antidiabetic; hepatoprotective; anticancer (azadirachtin under study).

Precautions: avoid in pregnancy/breastfeeding (may stimulate uterine contractions); neem oil is toxic if ingested by children under 12; caution with autoimmune conditions, fertility/conception, and long-term liver stress.
Common preparations include neem oil (external) and standardized capsules/tablets for internal detox and skin health.
The monograph cites peer-reviewed references including Biswas et al. (2002), Subapriya & Nagini (2005) and Alzohairy (2016).

qa.q: Why is Neem called the healer of all diseases?
qa.a: In Ayurveda Neem is called 'Sarva Roga Nivarini,' meaning healer of all diseases, because of its broad therapeutic range as a natural antimicrobial, blood purifier and detoxifier — and because nearly every part of the tree (leaf, bark, seed, oil, flower) is used medicinally.

qa.q: What are the main active compounds in Neem?
qa.a: Neem's key bioactives are azadirachtin (its signature limonoid, studied for antiparasitic and anti-proliferative effects) and nimbidin (anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial), along with flavonoids, tannins and fatty acids that are especially concentrated in neem oil.

qa.q: Is Neem safe to take?
qa.a: Neem is generally safe externally or in moderate doses, but it should be avoided during pregnancy and breastfeeding (it may stimulate uterine contractions), neem oil is toxic if ingested by children under 12, and caution applies with autoimmune conditions, fertility concerns, and long-term or excessive intake due to possible liver stress.

qa.q: What does research say about Neem?
qa.a: Modern research supports Neem's antibacterial action (against Staphylococcus aureus, Pseudomonas, E. coli and oral pathogens), antifungal action (Candida albicans, Aspergillus), antidiabetic blood-glucose-lowering potential, hepatoprotective effects, and anticancer/anti-proliferative activity of azadirachtin under study.

entries.title: Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri)
