facts: Bahera (Terminalia belerica Roxb.) is a large deciduous tree of the Combretaceae family, distinguished by its characteristic bark and obscurely 5-angled fruits, grown throughout India.
It is one of the three fruits of the Ayurvedic formulation Triphala, alongside Haritaki (Terminalia chebula) and Amalaki.
The herb is simultaneously astringent, tonic, expectorant and laxative, with activity attributed to polyphenolic and sterol constituents concentrated in the fruit.

Principal constituents include beta-sitosterol, gallic acid and ellagic acid, ethyl gallate and galloyl glucose (gallotannin-related), and chebulic acid (a marker polyphenol shared with Haritaki); the fruits possess antibacterial properties.

Traditional uses: coughs and sore throat (respiratory); fruit pulp for dropsy, piles and diarrhoea; laxative and general tonic; traditionally regarded as useful in leprosy and fever; and valued in hair-care preparations.
The fruit and pulp are the parts chiefly used, and it is most relevant today as a standardized fruit extract calibrated to its polyphenolic constituents for dose-consistent supply.
Available from GreenSpace Herbs as a standardized botanical extract for B2B supply.

qa.q: What are the active constituents of Terminalia belerica?
qa.a: Its fruit contains beta-sitosterol, the astringent polyphenols gallic acid and ellagic acid, gallotannin-related ethyl gallate and galloyl glucose, and chebulic acid (a marker polyphenol shared with Haritaki). These give the herb its astringency and antibacterial activity.

entries.title: Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia Miers)
entries.summary: Botanical extract monograph for Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia), Ayurveda's 'Amrita' — a Rasayana climbing shrub whose stem yields a standardized immunomodulatory, hepatoprotective and anti-inflammatory extract offered by GreenSpace Herbs for B2B supply.
facts: Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia Miers) belongs to the family Menispermaceae and is known in Ayurveda as Amrita, 'the immortal one'.
It is a large, glabrous, deciduous, perennial climbing shrub with succulent stems, heart-shaped leaves and aerial roots that often grow intertwined with trees such as neem or mango.
The stem is the chief medicinal part, though leaves and roots are also therapeutically relevant.

In Ayurveda it is Tikta (bitter) and Kashaya (astringent) in taste with Ushna virya (heating potency), balances all three doshas (Vata, Pitta, Kapha), and is described as medhya rasayana (brain tonic), jeevaniya (life-giving) and tridoshahara.

Key phytochemicals include alkaloids (tinosporine, magnoflorine, choline), diterpenoid lactones (tinosporide, cordifolide), glycosides (giloin, giloinin), steroids, polysaccharides and minor berberine.

It is found throughout tropical India (native also to Sri Lanka, Myanmar and Thailand), thriving in tropical/subtropical climates, sea level to about 1000 m, sandy loam or clayey soil, with 600–1500 mm annual rainfall.

Reported actions span immunomodulation and fever management (enhancing macrophage and lymphocyte activity), liver and detox support, antioxidant/anti-inflammatory action, diabetes and metabolic support (alpha-glucosidase inhibition), respiratory relief, and adaptogenic nervine support.
It is often combined with Tulsi or Kalmegh and used in Ayush Kadha and other herbal immune blends.

qa.q: What is Guduchi (giloy) used for?
qa.a: Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia), also called Amrita or giloy, is a Rasayana herb used as an immunomodulator, detoxifier and anti-inflammatory. It supports immune resilience, fever management, liver and detox function, antioxidant defense, balanced glucose metabolism, respiratory health and stress adaptation.

qa.q: What part of Guduchi is used and what is it standardized to?
qa.a: The stem is the chief medicinal part (leaves and roots are also used). GreenSpace Herbs offers it as a standardized botanical extract; its key actives are alkaloids such as tinosporine and magnoflorine, plus diterpenoid lactones and glycosides.

qa.q: Can I source Guduchi extract from GreenSpace Herbs?
qa.a: Yes. Guduchi is available as a standardized botanical extract for B2B supply. You can enquire or partner via the GreenSpace Herbs contact page.

entries.title: Chota Gokhru (Tribulus terrestris)
entries.summary: Detailed monograph for Chota Gokhru / Gokshura (Tribulus terrestris), a Zygophyllaceae herb standardized to 40–60% saponins (protodioscin) for urinary/renal, reproductive, hormonal, vitality and anti-inflammatory support; offered by GreenSpace Herbs as a standardized B2B botanical extract.
facts: Tribulus terrestris (Chota Gokhru), family Zygophyllaceae, is a prostrate, spreading annual herb with small pinnate leaves, yellow flowers and spiny burs; 'Gokhru' means cow's hoof ('Go' = cow, 'khura' = hoof).
Sanskrit/common names include Gokshura, Chota Gokhru and Puncture Vine; the whole plant is used, with emphasis on fruits and roots.
Ayurvedic profile: Rasa Madhura (sweet) and Tikta (bitter), Virya Sheeta (cooling), Vipaka Madhura; it pacifies Pitta and Vata doshas.
Its activity comes from steroidal saponins — protodioscin and protogracillin — plus flavonoids, alkaloids, glycosides and sterols; protodioscin is the signature bioactive.
Extracts are standardized for saponin content, typically 40–60%.

Uses span urinary/renal support (diuretic, dissolves renal and bladder calculi, used in Mutrakrichha, Ashmari, Mutraghata), reproductive/hormonal health (erectile function, testosterone, PCOS, ovulation), strength/vitality (lean muscle, endurance, a natural anabolic without hormonal suppression) and anti-inflammatory/metabolic support (gout, arthritis).
It appears in classical formulations Gokshuradi Guggulu and Punarnavadi Kashayam, and combines well with Guduchi and Punarnava.
Distributed across India, China, Australia, Sri Lanka and Southern Europe; hardy and drought-tolerant in warm, dry, semi-arid soils.
Precautions: not advised in pregnancy/lactation without guidance, mild hypotensive effect in low blood pressure, and caution in kidney impairment.

qa.q: What is Chota Gokhru (Tribulus terrestris) good for?
qa.a: Chota Gokhru is an Ayurvedic uroprotective and vitality herb. It acts as a diuretic that supports urinary tract health and helps dissolve kidney stones, modulates testosterone for male reproductive health, supports female reproductive health (PCOS, ovulation), and improves muscle strength, stamina and endurance.

qa.q: What is Tribulus terrestris standardized to?
qa.a: GreenSpace Herbs' Tribulus terrestris extract is standardized for saponin content, typically 40–60%. The key bioactive is the steroidal saponin protodioscin.

qa.q: Is Tribulus a steroid or does it suppress hormones?
qa.a: No. Tribulus acts as a natural anabolic that supports lean muscle mass and endurance without hormonal suppression. It should still be used with caution in pregnancy, low blood pressure and kidney impairment.

entries.title: Chota Gokru (Tribulus terrestris Linn.)

facts: Tribulus terrestris Linn., known as Chota Gokru (small Gokru) or Gokshura, is a prostrate annual herb of the family Zygophyllaceae, recognised by its ribbed, spinous, tuberculate tiny spherical fruits.
The fruits are the principal part used and the basis of the standardized extract.
The chief chemical constituents are steroidal sapogenins, which are the marker compounds used for identification and standardization.

Traditional uses: the fruits are diuretic and aphrodisiac, and clinical studies indicate an anti-urolithiatic property (action against urinary stone formation); it is also valued for muscle tone, stamina, endurance and increased sexual drive and performance.
Ayurvedic profile: Rasa Madhura (sweet), Virya Sheeta (cooling), Vipaka Madhura; it balances Vata and Pitta and is a rasayana for urinary health and vitality.
It is widely distributed in the tropics and warm temperate regions of India, China and parts of Europe, thriving in warm, dry, sunny conditions on poor, well-drained soils.

qa.q: What is Chota Gokru's key active compound?
qa.a: The chief constituents of Chota Gokru (Tribulus terrestris) are steroidal sapogenins, which are the marker compounds used to identify and standardize the extract.

qa.q: What does Tribulus terrestris fruit do?
qa.a: The spiny fruits are traditionally diuretic and aphrodisiac, and clinical studies indicate an anti-urolithiatic (anti-urinary-stone) property. The herb is also valued for improving muscle tone, stamina, endurance and sexual performance.

qa.q: Is this extract available for B2B supply?
qa.a: Yes. GreenSpace Herbs offers Tribulus terrestris (Chota Gokru) as a standardized botanical extract for B2B supply; enquire via the contact page.

entries.title: Fenugreek (Trigonella foenum-graecum)
entries.summary: Monograph for Fenugreek / Methi (Trigonella foenum-graecum), a Fabaceae seed botanical clinically supported for blood-sugar and cholesterol regulation, lactation and hormonal balance; offered by GreenSpace Herbs as a standardized seed extract for B2B supply.
facts: Fenugreek (Trigonella foenum-graecum), Ayurvedic name Methi or Methika, is a small annual herb of the family Fabaceae with trifoliate leaves and golden-yellow rhomboid seeds.
Ayurvedic profile: Rasa Tikta (bitter) and Kashaya (astringent), Virya Ushna (heating), Vipaka Katu (pungent); it primarily balances Kapha and Vata.

Seeds are the main medicinal part (leaves also used); key bioactives are diosgenin (steroidal saponin for hormone balance), trigonelline (hypoglycemic), 4-hydroxyisoleucine (stimulates insulin secretion) and galactomannan (soluble fiber).

Benefits include blood-sugar and metabolic support (reduces fasting and postprandial glucose in Type 2 diabetes, prediabetes and metabolic syndrome), cholesterol/cardiovascular support (lowers total cholesterol, LDL and triglycerides), women's health and lactation (galactagogue, PCOS relief), digestion/appetite control and anti-inflammatory action.

Clinical highlights: double-blind studies show significant reduction in HbA1c and fasting glucose; randomized trials confirm increased milk production; meta-analyses confirm reduced LDL and triglycerides.

It is native to the eastern Mediterranean and cultivated across Southern Europe, Western Asia, Sri Lanka and India (Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, Uttar Pradesh); grown in the rabi (winter) season as a 90–120 day crop.

Precautions: high doses may stimulate uterine contractions in pregnancy, it may enhance diabetes medication effects (monitor blood sugar), caution in hypothyroidism, and it can cause a maple-syrup body odor due to sotolone.

qa.q: What is fenugreek (methi) used for?
qa.a: Fenugreek supports blood-sugar and metabolic regulation, lowers cholesterol (LDL and triglycerides), enhances milk production in nursing mothers (galactagogue), aids digestion and appetite control, and helps hormonal balance including PCOS symptom relief.

qa.q: What are the key active compounds in fenugreek?
qa.a: The main bioactives are diosgenin (a steroidal saponin for hormone balance), trigonelline (hypoglycemic action), 4-hydroxyisoleucine (stimulates insulin secretion) and galactomannan (a soluble fiber for blood sugar and appetite control).

qa.q: Is fenugreek clinically proven for diabetes?
qa.a: Clinical research supports it: double-blind studies show significant reductions in HbA1c and fasting glucose. Because it may enhance the effect of diabetes medication, blood sugar should be monitored when combining them.

entries.title: Fenugreek (Trigonella foenum-graecum Linn.)
entries.summary: Monograph for Fenugreek / Methi (Trigonella foenum-graecum Linn.), a Fabaceae seed botanical standardized for saponins/galactomannan and clinically supported for blood-sugar, lipid and lactation support; offered by GreenSpace Herbs as a standardized B2B seed extract.
facts: Fenugreek (Trigonella foenum-graecum Linn.), Ayurvedic name Methi/Methika, is a short-duration leguminous herb of the family Fabaceae with trifoliate leaves and golden-yellow rhomboid seeds.
Ayurvedic profile: Rasa Tikta and Kashaya, Virya Ushna, Vipaka Katu; it primarily balances Kapha and Vata.
Seeds are the main medicinal part (leaves also used); key bioactives are diosgenin, trigonelline, 4-hydroxyisoleucine and galactomannan, plus alkaloids, flavonoids and polyphenols.

Extract is standardized as a seed extract for saponins/galactomannan; benefits include enhanced insulin sensitivity and secretion, lower fasting/postprandial glucose, reduced total cholesterol, LDL and triglycerides, improved HDL, galactagogue/lactation support, PCOS relief, and appetite/digestive support.

Native to Western Asia, the Mediterranean and the Indian subcontinent; cultivated in India (Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, Uttar Pradesh), North Africa, the Mediterranean, China, the Middle East, Southern Europe and Sri Lanka.
Growing conditions: cool dry rabi (winter) season, up to 1800 m altitude, loamy/sandy soil with neutral pH, 500–1000 mm annual rainfall, 90–120 day crop cycle.
Clinical highlights: double-blind reduction in HbA1c and fasting glucose; randomized trials confirm increased milk production; meta-analyses confirm reduced LDL and triglycerides.

Precautions: caution in pregnancy (uterine contractions at high doses), with diabetes medication (monitor blood sugar), and in thyroid-sensitive individuals; possible maple-syrup body odor from sotolone.

qa.q: What is fenugreek standardized to?
qa.a: GreenSpace Herbs offers fenugreek as a standardized seed extract for saponins/galactomannan. Its named actives are diosgenin, trigonelline, 4-hydroxyisoleucine and galactomannan.

qa.q: What formulations use fenugreek extract?
qa.a: As a clean-label active it is used in metabolic-health, blood-sugar, weight-management and lactation-support products — postpartum tonics, satiety and appetite blends, and women's wellness formulations — delivered as seed extract, powder, tea or capsule.

qa.q: How does fenugreek help blood sugar?
qa.a: Its soluble galactomannan fiber slows carbohydrate absorption while trigonelline and 4-hydroxyisoleucine enhance insulin sensitivity and secretion, lowering fasting and postprandial glucose. Clinical studies show reduced HbA1c and fasting glucose.

entries.title: Haritaki (Terminalia chebula)
entries.summary: Botanical extract monograph for Haritaki (Terminalia chebula Retz.), family Combretaceae, the tannin-rich Ayurvedic "king of medicines" and chief fruit of Triphala, standardized to chebulagic and chebulic acids for digestive, immune and liver support.
facts: Haritaki is botanically Terminalia chebula Retz. of the family Combretaceae, a large deciduous tree whose dried ribbed fruit (pericarp) is the medicinally active material used for extraction.
It is the chief of the three fruits in the classical Ayurvedic formulation Triphala, alongside Bibhitaki (Terminalia bellerica) and Amalaki (Emblica officinalis).
Sanskrit name Haritaki; Hindi name Harada; revered in Ayurveda as the "king of medicines."
Principal constituents include chebulagic acid, chebulic acid, corilagin, gallic and ellagic acids, tannins and flavonoid glycosides; the extract is standardized to chebulagic and chebulic acids.
Traditional and studied actions: gentle regulating laxative and stomachic, immunomodulatory support, and hepatoprotective/antioxidant activity protecting liver tissue against oxidative stress.

Ayurvedic properties: Rasa predominantly Kashaya (astringent) plus Amla, Katu, Tikta and Madhura; Virya Ushna (heating); Vipaka Madhura (sweet); tridoshic, especially pacifying to Vata — carries five of the six tastes (all except salty).
Native to South and Southeast Asia including India (sub-Himalayan tracts and Deccan plateau), Nepal, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Myanmar, Thailand and southern China; thrives up to ~1,500 metres.
Traditionally avoided during pregnancy, in severe dehydration, and in states of exhaustion or excess heat; available as a standardized botanical extract for B2B supply.

qa.q: What is Haritaki good for?
qa.a: Haritaki (Terminalia chebula) supports digestion as a gentle regulating laxative and stomachic, promotes healthy bowel movement without harsh purging, and provides immunomodulatory, antioxidant and hepatoprotective (liver-protecting) support. It is used as a Rasayana rejuvenative and is the chief fruit of Triphala.

qa.q: What is Haritaki standardized to?
qa.a: GreenSpace's Haritaki extract is standardized to its characteristic hydrolysable tannins — chebulagic acid and chebulic acid — with corilagin, gallic and ellagic acids also present.

qa.q: How is Haritaki related to Triphala?
qa.a: Haritaki is the principal (chief) ingredient of Triphala, the classic equal-part Ayurvedic blend combining Haritaki with Bibhitaki (Terminalia bellerica) and Amalaki (Emblica officinalis).

qa.q: Who should avoid Haritaki?
qa.a: It is traditionally avoided during pregnancy, in severe dehydration, and in states of exhaustion or excess heat. As with any potent botanical, dosage should be guided by a qualified practitioner.

entries.title: Haritaki (Terminalia chebula)
entries.summary: Detailed monograph for Haritaki (Terminalia chebula), the Ayurvedic "King of Medicines" — a tannin-rich Rasayana fruit standardized as a B2B extract for gut health, detox, cognition, metabolic support and longevity, with cited research references.
facts: Haritaki (Terminalia chebula) is called the "King of Medicines" (Kayastha) and is revered across Ayurveda, Siddha, Unani and Tibetan medicine as a restorative and longevity agent.
It is classified as Rasa Panchaka, carrying five of the six tastes (all except salty), and is one of the three fruits of Triphala.
The mature dried fruit pericarp is the principal part used, collected in autumn and sun-dried before powdering or extraction.

Active constituents include chebulinic and chebulagic acids, gallic and ellagic acids, tannins (20–40%), flavonoids, alkaloids, terpenoids and ascorbic acid (Vitamin C); the aside lists standardization to Tannins (20–40%).

Benefits span digestion/detox (enhances Agni, relieves constipation, used in IBS and bloating), rejuvenation/longevity, cognition (Medhya Rasayana brain tonic), oral/eye/skin care, and metabolic support (improves insulin sensitivity, reduces postprandial glucose).

Research highlights: enhances colonic motility without dependency; chebulinic acid protects neurons from oxidative damage; anti-diabetic glucose control; antibacterial/antifungal against E. coli, Staphylococcus aureus and Candida albicans; promotes collagen synthesis and wound healing.

Grows across India (Western Ghats, Himalayan foothills, Central and Eastern forests), Nepal, Bhutan, Sri Lanka, Myanmar and Bangladesh; sea level up to roughly 1,500 m in tropical/subtropical deciduous forest.

Precautions: avoid high doses in pregnancy (uterine-stimulation risk), in severe debility/emaciation (may increase dryness), and in diarrhea or dehydration; cited references include Saleem 2002 (hepatoprotective) and Bag 2009 (antidiabetic).

qa.q: Why is Haritaki called the King of Medicines?
qa.a: Haritaki (Terminalia chebula) is called the "King of Medicines" (Kayastha) in Ayurveda because of its broad rejuvenating, digestive and detoxifying action across many body systems, and because it uniquely carries five of the six tastes (all except salty).

qa.q: Does Haritaki help with brain and memory?
qa.a: Yes. Haritaki is used as a Medhya Rasayana (brain tonic) to enhance memory, concentration and neuroplasticity, and is considered beneficial in anxiety, stress and early cognitive decline. Its chebulinic acid has been shown to protect neurons from oxidative damage.

qa.q: Is Haritaki backed by research?
qa.a: Yes. Research supports its laxative/digestive action (colonic motility without dependency), neuroprotection, anti-diabetic glucose control, antibacterial and antifungal activity (against E. coli, S. aureus, Candida albicans), and wound healing via collagen synthesis. The page cites five journal references.

qa.q: What forms of Haritaki are available?
qa.a: It is used as powder (Churna, taken with warm water, honey or ghee), capsules/tablets, and decoction (Kashayam), and is available as a standardized botanical extract for B2B supply.

The framework is branded "Quantum Ayurveda," positioning energetic homeostasis and re-tuning of the body's resonant structures as a path to sustainable longevity.

entries.title: Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia)
entries.summary: B2B extract monograph for Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia), family Menispermaceae — the Ayurvedic Rasayana "Amrita" — with clinically supported immunomodulatory, antioxidant, anti-inflammatory and adaptogenic activity, characterized bioactives, mechanism of action, and regulatory/sustainability details.
facts: Guduchi is Tinospora cordifolia, a large perennial climbing shrub of the Menispermaceae family, indigenous to tropical and subtropical India; the stem is the principal medicinal part (root and leaf also used).
Its Sanskrit names are Guduchi ("that which protects the body") and Amrita ("nectar of immortality"); it is classified in Ayurveda as a Rasayana (rejuvenative).
Characterized bioactives include tinosporide and tinocordiside (diterpenoid/glycoside markers), the alkaloids magnoflorine and berberine, sinapic acid and syringin, and β-sitosterol.

Mechanisms: immunomodulation via cytokine regulation and T-/B-lymphocyte activation; anti-inflammatory suppression of NF-κB; antioxidant boosting of SOD, catalase and glutathione peroxidase; adaptogenic reduction of stress biomarkers.
Typical dosage of standardized extracts is 300–1000 mg/day; formats include powders, capsules, decoctions, liquid concentrates, functional teas, tonics and health shots.

Regulatory status: FSSAI-approved for nutraceuticals and Ayurvedic medicines in India; included in US botanical dietary supplements under DSHEA; listed in WHO monographs for traditional medicinal use with an established safety profile.
Sustainably wild-harvested and cultivated in India under agroforestry, with climbing stems collected without uprooting to preserve renewable supply; produced under GMP-standard processing.
Traditionally grows over host trees such as neem and mango and is used for managing fevers, enhancing infection resistance and supporting recovery from chronic illness.

qa.q: What does Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) do?
qa.a: Guduchi is an Ayurvedic Rasayana known as Amrita ("nectar of immortality") with clinically supported immunomodulatory, antioxidant, anti-inflammatory and adaptogenic benefits. It works by regulating cytokines and lymphocytes, suppressing NF-κB, boosting antioxidant enzymes (SOD, catalase, glutathione peroxidase), and reducing stress biomarkers.

qa.q: What is Guduchi extract standardized to and dosed at?
qa.a: GreenSpace's Guduchi is a standardized extract characterized for terpenoid/alkaloid markers such as tinosporide, tinocordiside, magnoflorine and berberine, with typical dosages of 300–1000 mg/day.

qa.q: Is Tinospora cordifolia approved for supplements?
qa.a: Yes. It is FSSAI-approved for nutraceuticals and Ayurvedic medicines in India, included in US botanical dietary supplements under DSHEA, and listed in WHO monographs for traditional medicinal use with an established safety profile.

qa.a: It is wild-harvested and cultivated in India under sustainable agroforestry, with the climbing stems collected without uprooting the plants to preserve ecological balance and ensure a renewable supply.

entries.title: Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia)
entries.summary: Detailed monograph for Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia / Amrita / Giloy), family Menispermaceae — a tridoshic Rasayana standardized to 2.5% bitters, covering phytochemistry, immune/liver/metabolic/respiratory benefits, dosage forms, research highlights and precautions with cited references.
facts: Guduchi is Tinospora cordifolia (family Menispermaceae), a perennial climbing shrub with heart-shaped leaves and aerial roots that grows intertwined with trees like neem and mango; celebrated in Ayurveda as Amrita ("the immortal one") and a top Rasayana.
Ayurvedic properties: Tikta (bitter) and Kashaya (astringent) taste with Ushna virya (heating potency); balances all three doshas.

The stem is the chief medicinal part (leaves and roots also used); key phytochemicals include alkaloids (tinosporine, magnoflorine, choline), diterpenoid lactones (tinosporide, cordifolide), glycosides (giloin, giloinin), steroids, polysaccharides and minor berberine.
The extract is standardized to 2.5% Bitters (per the Quick Facts panel).

Benefits cover immune/fever support (natural febrifuge, component of Ayush Kadha), liver protection/detox (fatty liver, hepatitis, jaundice), anti-inflammatory/antioxidant action (rheumatoid arthritis, gout, lupus), metabolic support (alpha-glucosidase inhibition reducing postprandial glucose) and respiratory relief.

Research highlights: increased white blood cell activity; protection against CCl4- and alcohol-induced liver damage in animal models; reduced fasting glucose and improved glycemic control in Type 2 diabetes; double-blind support for reduced RA pain/inflammation; inclusion in AYUSH COVID-19 supportive-care guidelines.
Dosage forms include Powder (Churna), Guduchi Satva (starchy extract for chronic fevers), tablets/capsules, decoction (Kashayam) and fresh stem juice (Swarasa).

Precautions: limited high-dose data in pregnancy/lactation; use under supervision in autoimmune disease (can stimulate immune activity); monitor blood sugar when combined with anti-diabetic medications; cited references include Kapil & Sharma 1997 and Gupta 2016.

qa.q: What is Guduchi standardized to?
qa.a: GreenSpace's Guduchi (Tinospora cordifolia) extract is standardized to 2.5% Bitters. Its key phytochemicals include alkaloids (tinosporine, magnoflorine, choline), diterpenoid lactones (tinosporide, cordifolide) and glycosides (giloin, giloinin).

qa.q: Is Guduchi good for immunity and fever?
qa.a: Yes. Guduchi is a well-researched immunomodulator shown to increase white blood cell activity, and it acts as a natural febrifuge useful in viral and chronic fevers. It is a frequent component of Ayush Kadha and immune blends and was included in AYUSH guidelines for immune resilience during COVID-19.

qa.q: Does Guduchi help the liver and blood sugar?
qa.a: Yes. It is hepatoprotective — protecting against CCl4-, alcohol- and drug-induced liver damage and used in fatty liver, hepatitis and jaundice — and it supports glucose metabolism via alpha-glucosidase inhibition, reducing fasting glucose and improving glycemic control in Type 2 diabetes.

qa.q: Who should be cautious with Guduchi?
qa.a: People who are pregnant or lactating (limited high-dose data), those with autoimmune diseases (it can stimulate immune activity, so use under supervision), and those on anti-diabetic medications (monitor blood sugar for hypoglycemia).
